data.title: 'Katherine Mansfield' Review: The Woolf of Wellington
data.description:
By Kathryn Hughes

The first meeting between Virginia Woolf and Katherine Mansfield did not go well. She "stinks like a — well civet cat that had taken to street walking," Woolf spluttered to her diary on Oct. 11, 1917. (Woolf was objecting to Mansfield's wearing scent.) She added that Mansfield, a native New Zealander, was "hard & cheap." Though the diary entry concedes that Mansfield was "so intelligent & inscrutable that she repays friendship," the two women wouldn't become friends. Rather, as Gerri Kimber explains in "Katherine Mansfield: A Hidden Life," they would be known as literary sparring partners, both engaged in the high-stakes project of forging a new modernist literature. In their fiction — mostly novels for Woolf, short stories for Mansfield — they experimented by replacing linear narrative and descriptive detail with suggestion and symbolism. Instead of omniscient narrators, they deployed free indirect speech to reveal characters' innermost thoughts. Years later Woolf admitted that Mansfield was the only writer she had ever felt jealous of.

Mansfield had settled permanently in Britain at 19, in 1908. On the run from her well-to-do, stuffy colonial background, she launched herself into London's bohemia. While still in New Zealand, suffocating in suburban Wellington, she had described herself as a "restless soul" with "a rapacious appetite for everything and principles as light as my purse." Now set free, Mansfield seized every experience going. She wrote a little, acted a little, sang a little. She also had a lot of sex, believing that "the idea of sitting still and waiting for a husband is absolutely revolting."

As it turned out, she didn't have to wait long. A year after her arrival, Mansfield married George Bowden, a singing teacher she had met a month before. The omens were not good. The bride wore black and, pregnant with another man's child, made a point of spending the wedding night elsewhere. Recently discovered divorce papers reveal that Bowden blamed the marriage breakdown on Mansfield's "insatiable desires" for sex, which he was unable to satisfy.

Mansfield's family was convinced that she was a lesbian (one of her most intense early affairs had been with a M ori schoolfriend), and believed this was why her marriage to Bowden had faltered. Her mother arrived from New Zealand to sweep her off to a Bavarian spa whose waters were said to cure homosexuality; it was in Bad Wörishofen that Mansfield first read Chekhov and began to write seriously. She produced several short stories, including "Frau Brechenmacher Attends a Wedding," which explores the dynamics within marriage. These appeared in her acclaimed first collection, "In a German Pension" (1911).

Although she never returned to New Zealand, the country of her birth provided Mansfield with her best material. After the death of her beloved younger brother in World War I, she lived imaginatively in the landscape of their shared childhood. The stories she wrote after 1915 — "Prelude," "At the Bay," "The Garden Party" — are among her very best, full of vividly recalled sights, sounds and even scents.

In "The Garden Party" (1922), Mansfield demonstrated her mastery of what today might be called a "cold open." It begins: "And after all the weather was ideal," plunging the reader headfirst into the collision between preparations for a party and funeral preparations for a young man killed in an accident.

Mansfield met her second husband, the editor John Middleton Murry, when she submitted a short story to his avant-garde magazine, Rhythm. They embarked on a painfully destructive intermittent relationship, which culminated in an unhappy marriage in 1918. Both were spectacularly unfaithful, but Murry's bad behavior was compounded by psychological sadism. On one occasion, after Mansfield had been diagnosed with tuberculosis and fled to the French Riviera in an attempt to heal, Murry let slip that he had discussed setting up home with his mistress, the aristocratic Dorothy Brett.

Virginia Woolf went on to publish modernist masterworks including "Mrs. Dalloway" (1925) and "The Waves" (1931); Mansfield never lived to do so. Her tuberculosis killed her in 1923, at the age of 34. Ignoring Mansfield's dying instructions "to tear up and burn as much as possible, " Murry published his wife's journals and letters, editing them to ensure that she emerged as a figure of romantic tragedy. All evidence of Mansfield's busy sex life, not to mention her malicious wit and suicidal episodes, were excised. Naturally, the unhappiness of her marriage to Murry was nowhere in sight. Contemporaries were convinced that his motivation for publishing Mansfield's ephemera wasn't so much reverence for her talent as greed for hard cash. The critic Sylvia Lynd accused Murry of "boiling Katherine's bones to make soup"; D.H. Lawrence said he "made capital out of her death."

Since the late 1970s, a number of biographies of Mansfield have sought to dismantle the sanitized version of her life promoted by Murry, and a scholarly industry devoted to restoring her unadulterated voice has flourished. Ms. Kimber, a visiting professor of English literature at the University of Northampton, has dedicated her academic career to ferreting out overlooked documents, and it is one such discovery — ironically not by her — that she makes the central plank of this biography.

Drawing on work by the scholar John Wood, Ms. Kimber proposes that Murry was not Mansfield's most significant emotional and professional relationship; that claim properly belongs to a man called A.R. Orage, editor of the New Age magazine, which published some of her earliest stories. In addition to documenting Orage's literary mentoring of the young Mansfield, Ms. Kimber claims that the two had an affair. The evidence for this seems slim. More certain is that Orage stayed in touch with Mansfield throughout her life. He introduced her to the teachings of George Gurdjieff, the esoteric spiritual teacher who took Western intellectual circles by storm in the 1920s. Mansfield was "fascinated" by Gurdjieff's ideas, according to Ms. Kimber; when she died, she was at the Institute for the Harmonious Development of Man in Fontainebleau, outside Paris. She had gone there in a last-ditch attempt to recover her health and instead collapsed with a violent pulmonary hemorrhage.

This discovery of Orage's significance in Katherine Mansfield's life and work matters to scholars, but Ms. Kimber misjudges how much it might engross readers of a biography. Her preoccupation with archival minutiae fills space that could have been better spent. The reader doesn't discover much about how Mansfield's work was shaped by changing personal circumstances, from hectic bohemia to a series of continental spas, and learns too little about Mansfield's achievements as a literary provocateur. Katherine Mansfield could write wonderful lines such as: "The men walked like scissors; the women trod like cats." I finished this book still wanting to know how she did it.

  • Ms. Hughes is the author of "Catland: Louise Wain and the Great Cat Mania."

data.description:
RNS Number : 1719R Templeton Emerging Markets IT PLC 30 January 2026  

Stock Exchange Announcement

Templeton Emerging Markets Investment Trust PLC ("the Company")

Transaction in Own Shares

Legal Entity Identifier 5493002NMTB70RZBXO96

The Company announces that, on 30 January 2026, it purchased for cancellation 91,213 Ordinary shares of 5 pence each in the Company ("Shares") at a price of 263.00 pence per Share.

Following this transaction, the Company's share capital consists of 954,148,611 Shares in issue and 60,000,000 Shares held in Treasury. Therefore, the total number of voting rights in the Company is 954,148,611. In order to protect the interests of existing shareholders, shares held in treasury will only be reissued at a price above the prevailing NAV per share at the time of reissue.

This figure (954,148,611) may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

For further information please e-mail temitcosec@franklintempleton.com.

data.title: CVLG: Freight revenue up, margins down; Star acquisition and market recovery expected to drive 2026 gains
data.description:
Freight revenue grew 7.8% year-over-year, but adjusted operating income fell 39.4% due to margin compression. The Star Logistics acquisition diversifies revenue and is expected to be accretive in 2026. Management is optimistic about improved market conditions and margin recovery later in the year.

data.title: Chevron CEO says can process another 100,000 bpd of Venezuelan crude at its refineries
data.description:
Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said on Friday the U.S. oil major can process an additional 100,000 barrels per day of Venezuelan crude at its refineries.

The company currently processes 50,000 barrels a day of Venezuelan crude at its Pascagoula refinery on the U.S. Gulf Coast, Wirth said.

data.title: FTSE MIB Rises on Strong GDP and Bank Gains
data.description:
The FTSE MIB rose 1% to close at 45,527 on Friday, tracking gains for European stocks on evidence of traction in the continent's growth.

Both the Eurozone and the domestic GDPs grew by 0.3% in the fourth quarter amid resilience in private consumption, lifting the outlook for corporate returns.

Banks benefited from the positive credit prospective with Intesa Sanpaolo, UniCredit, and Mediobanca adding between 1.7% and 3%.

Most luxury names also outperformed, with Moncler up 1.3%, Brunello Cucinelli advancing 1.9% and Campari gaining 3%.

Other notable gainers included STMicroelectronics (+1.3%), Stellantis (+2.0%) and Prysmian (+1.2%).

On the downside, Ferrari (-0.8%) and Eni (-0.9%) both ended the session lower.

data.title: How China's Military Robots Learn From Animals — WSJ
data.description:
By Conor Grant

This is an edition of The Future of Everything newsletter, a look at how innovation and technology are transforming the way we live, work and play. If you're not subscribed, sign up here .

After observing how hawks target their prey, engineers at one of China's top military-linked universities trained defensive drones to single out and destroy vulnerable enemy aircraft. On the opposite side, these scientists used the flight behavior of doves to train the attacking drones how to dodge their hawk-trained adversaries.

This week, Josh Chin reports on how China is using insights about animal behavior to train AI-controlled drones.

As drones have become cheaper and more capable, sci-fi visions of AI-powered robot armies clashing on the battlefield have inched closer to reality.

Beijing's military is focused on swarming drones, which it hopes can usher in a new era of unmanned warfighting — while also capitalizing on China's advantage in hardware.

How long it took five "hawk" drones to destroy five "dove" drones, in a test conducted at China's Beihang University School of Automation Research and Electrical Engineering

Recent research into drone intelligence is yielding new algorithms modeled on the behavior of several animal groups — including ants, sheep, coyotes and whales — that could theoretically give drones rules for how to coordinate with one another. Few of these new algorithms have been tested in realistic battlefield scenarios.

More on this topic:

  • Modern tech and old-school spycraft are redefining war. (Read)
  • Taiwan is looking to new sea-drone tech to repel China. (Read)
  • The Pentagon can't trust GPS anymore. Is quantum physics the answer? (Read)

🤔 Do you think drones will transform warfare? Why or why not? Send me your thoughts, questions and predictions at future@wsj.com (if you're reading this in your inbox, you can just hit reply).

More of What's Next: Neolabs; Chinese EVs; Voice Computing

These billion-dollar AI labs have no products, no revenue...and eager investors. Startups that some people are calling "neolabs," which prioritize long-term research and AI development over immediate profits, are attracting significant venture capital.

Joanna Stern drove a Chinese EV — and never wants to buy an American car again. The WSJ columnist spent two weeks test-driving a Xiaomi SU7. Though the car isn't available for purchase in the states, it's still freaking out U.S. automakers.

Our gadgets finally speak human, and tech will never be the same, writes Christopher Mims. Millions of people across the world are now regularly talking to their devices, an interaction that has become more productive — and less annoying — thanks to AI.

From faster-drying paint to better-smelling soap, AI is trying product development. Large companies including Procter & Gamble, PPG and 3M are using digital tools to speed up their testing processes and suggest counterintuitive solutions.

Future Feedback

Last week, we reported on scientists who are developing a new way to track falling space debris using sonic booms. Readers shared their thoughts on the dangers of space debris:

  • "I'm concerned about the risks of falling space debris, but still optimistic that this is a solvable problem if we take orbital safety seriously. Despite numerous operational improvements, growth in the industry is outpacing governance. Though the risk to people on the ground is still low, re-entries are increasing -- and transparency should keep pace. I expect more incidents will pressure the system, and I hope they push us toward stronger rules before a major preventable event forces a change." -- Enrique Sánchez-Rivera, Arizona
  • "Not nearly enough has been done about debris in space. The industry had to be aware of how much debris was being created and should have made a conscious effort to avoid creating excessive debris. This is a problem that could have been avoided." -- Council Mattoon, Kansas
  • "My question is: Why do we think the chance of space collisions is a big problem? If I'm in one spot in my county and my wife is at another other end of it, and we're driving without any knowledge of each other or anything in our way, what are the chances we'd hit each other? What would the chances be if we were in the parking lot of a Walmart? I think the chances are still small. Space debris and satellites are often at different altitudes, going in different directions -- and they have a large area to cover. I've never understood why space collisions are considered such a danger." -- Tim McBrayer, Ohio

(Responses have been condensed and edited.)

Elsewhere in the Future

  • Researchers are using AI to decode the human genome. (The New York Times)
  • Meet the hardcore longevity enthusiasts who believe death is "wrong." (MIT Technology Review)
  • Rebecca Solnit writes about what technology takes from us -- and how to get it back. (The Guardian)

About Us

Thanks for reading The Future of Everything. We cover the innovation and tech transforming the way we live, work and play. This newsletter was written by Conor Grant. Get in touch with us at future@wsj.com. Got a tip for us? Here's how to submit.

See more from The Future of Everything at wsj.com/future-of-everything.

data.title: Week's Best: Get In on Gold and Silver? — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Barron's Advisor Staff

Precious metals have been on a tear lately. Investor concerns about geopolitical risks, the economy, and the weaker dollar have propelled gold to record highs. Meanwhile, silver, driven by industrial demand, has also soared. For this week's Barron's Advisor Big Q, we asked wealth management professionals whether it is too late for investors to get in on these rallies. Are further gains ahead, or will new investors be left holding the bag?

Among other most-read wealth management articles this week:

Morgan Stanley's top planner on longevity risk . Financial planning is far more complex today than in years past, partly because people are living longer and because there are many more investment opportunities available. Successful multigenerational planning requires buy-in from all family members, says Anthea Tjuanakis Cox, head of financial planning at Morgan Stanley. She discusses why the traditional retirement model is becoming obsolete and why advisors should embrace scenario planning.

Commonwealth advisors are exiting LPL, report says . When LPL Financial said it would acquire rival wealth manager Commonwealth Financial Network last March, Commonwealth's approximately 2,900 advisors had a choice: stay with LPL or find a new home. A new report sheds light on how many Commonwealth advisors have headed for the exits.

New wealth leaders at Goldman . Goldman Sachs is shuffling the ranks of its wealth and asset management leadership and adding seven leaders from that side of the business to the firm's powerful management committee, the company said Monday. The appointments to the management committee effectively elevate the status of the wealth and asset management business within the company.

What gray divorce means for wealth transfer . The great wealth transfer is often framed as a function of longevity and inheritance: baby boomers aging, assets passing at death, and women ultimately controlling more wealth because they outlive men. Financial advisors who see the horizontal asset transfer as a postmortem event, however, may miss the moment when money actually moves into the hands of Gen X and baby boomer women.

Time to work together with tax preparers . Wealth management firms such as Konvergent are capitalizing on a nationwide shortage of accounting professionals by positioning themselves as strategic partners who can help accountants deliver more sophisticated planning to clients. Advisors benefit when accountants refer clients, but the most fruitful partnerships go much deeper than a simple handoff.

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Uber Eats Ordered to Pay $3.5M Over NYC Delivery Worker Pay
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Uber Technologies' food-delivery business agreed to pay $3.5 million in worker pay restitution and civil penalties as part of an order by the New York City government.

New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani and the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection ordered Uber Eats to pay $3.15 million in restitution to 48,000 workers across the city, and $350,000 in civil penalties and fees.

An investigation by DCWP alleged that Uber Eats failed to pay workers the minimum pay rate between December 2023 and September 2024 for time spent on canceled trips, the mayor's office said Friday.

A DCWP report said Uber and other food delivery companies engineered their interfaces to lower workers' tip earnings by $550 million.

Food delivery platforms Fantuan and HungryPanda are also being ordered to make payments alongside Uber Eats for a total amount of $5.2 million.

Uber also agreed to reinstate workers who were wrongfully deactivated, which could be as many as 10,000 people, the mayor's office said.

Earlier in January, DCWP Commissioner Sam Levine sent compliance warnings to more than 60 delivery companies including Uber, Instacart, DoorDash and Grubhub. The notices warned companies to adhere to expanded delivery worker protection laws that went into effect in January.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Schwab Taps Neesha Hathi for New Role Leading Combined Wealth and Banking Operations — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

Charles Schwab is shuffling the ranks of its executive leadership and bringing together its wealth and banking businesses. The company named Neesha Hathi to head the new Wealth Advisory and Banking Services division, effective July 1.

Hathi, who currently serves as managing director and head of wealth and advice solutions, will continue to report to Schwab President and CEO Rick Wurster.

The union of the wealth unit and banking, with its profitable lending business, brings together two key growth areas for the company. Schwab says the combined operation will "focus on deepening relationships with individual investors and advisors through expanded wealth advisory and lending capabilities."

Schwab executives have noted that both in-house wealth capabilities and lending are priorities for the company, and areas where Citizens analyst Devin Ryan says Schwab is "meaningfully under penetrated."

Schwab announced a series of other executive transitions. Paul Woolway, the CEO of Charles Schwab Bank, intends to retire July 1. At that point, Tyler Woulfe, who currently serves as managing director of banking and trust services, will take over as president and CEO of the bank, reporting to Hathi.

Mitch Mantua, Schwab's general auditor, is also planning to retire July 1. Jessica Bramhall will take that position, a promotion from her current role as managing director of corporate internal audit operations. Schwab says that she will report both to the company's audit committee and to Wurster.

On the IT side of the operation, Schwab is creating a Technology, Operations, and Data division, to be headed by Dennis Howard. The operations side of that unit is new — Howard currently oversees technology and data. He will continue to report to Wurster.

Wurster says the personnel transitions are a testament to the "strength and depth of our leadership bench" and thanked Woolway and Mantua for their service to the company. "These moves position us to continue delivering for the individuals, families, and businesses who trust Schwab with their financial lives," he says.

Separately, Schwab on Thursday announced a 19% dividend increase. Shares were trading around $103 late Friday morning, off about 0.3%.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: LyondellBasell Posts FY Loss on Higher Costs, Asset Write-Downs — OPIS
data.description:
U.S. petrochemical major LyondellBasell has posted a loss of $738 million for 2025, a significant swing into the red from the net income of $1.37 billion it made just twelve months earlier.

Still, LyondellBasell reported a Q4 2025 net loss of $140 million, according to its published results Friday, a much improved result on its Q4 2024 $603 million loss.

The company blamed the Q4 income losses on asset write-downs, costs incurred for transactions, its Cash Improvement Plan and discontinued operations.

Poor Q4 margins on higher costs for NGL feedstocks and natural gas were also cited. Higher costs linked to maintenance and seasonally softer demand that kept a lid on its prices also had an impact. In North America, integrated polyethylene margins were reduced due to firmer upstream input costs.

Polyethylene production fell in part due to the maintenance as well as the failing seasonal demand in both Europe and North America.

Headwinds from global trade disruptions to petrochemical markets, falling oil prices and new capacity additions that outpaced global demand growth also eroded profits for the chemicals major.

In North America and Europe, polyethylene and polymer segment margins were impacted by trade issues, higher feedstock costs and ample supply, while import competition also dented European polymer margins, it said. A $1.2 billion impairment charge was recorded in Q3, which included European asset write-downs, was driven by weak consumer sentiment.

Further capex planned after strategic European review

In June 2025, LyondellBasell struck a deal with German private equity group AEQUITA to sell facilities based in France, Germany, Spain and the U.K., after a strategic European review announced in 2024.

LyondellBasell's facility in Berre, France, has an ethylene capacity of around 450,000 metric tons/year and the German Munchmunster site has a 400,000 mt/year capacity. The U.K.'s Carrington plant produces around 200,000 mt/year of polypropylene, while in Spain, Tarragona's output is approximately 400,000 mt/year of polypropylene, according to data from Chemical Market Analytics by OPIS, a Dow Jones company.

"During an exceptionally challenging environment, we focused on safety, operational excellence and cash conversion to advance progress on our long-term goals," said CEO Peter Vanacker.

"We moved forward on the three pillars of our strategy and made material progress toward optimizing our business portfolio. At the same time, we took actions to preserve value by shifting the timing of certain elements of our strategy, including limiting our investments in circularity and sustainability to markets with proven and resilient demand."

The company said it plans to invest $1.2 billion in capital expenditure this year. It will focus on maintaining safe, reliable operations while continuing the construction of MoReTec-1 at its Wesseling, Germany site.

When market conditions improve, and the industry moves out of the downcycle, LyondellBasell said it has a pipeline of projects to grow long-term value.

These include its low-cost propylene capacity with Flex-2 and increasing chemical recycling capacity with MoReTec-2, both at the Houston facility, while also expanding cost-advantaged Middle East production, the company said in the statement.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Reporting by Miguel Cambeiro, mcambeiro@opisnet.com; Editing by Rob Sheridan, rsheridan@opisnet.com

data.title: RARE Stock Slips 3% Today — What’s Up With Ultragenyx’s Gene Therapy For Sanfilippo Syndrome?
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ultragenyx now expects a 6-month review period for the therapy and a likely decision from the FDA regarding potential approval by the third quarter of 2026.
  • The FDA refused to approve the company’s application for the drug in July, and issued a letter requesting Ultragenyx to provide more information.
  • However, no date has been officially provided by the agency.

Shares of Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical Inc. (RARE) fell 3% on Friday after the company said that it has submitted an application to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) regarding a drug previously refused approval.

The company said that it has resubmitted an application for UX111 gene therapy as a treatment for patients with Sanfilippo syndrome type A.

Fresh Submission Details

The fresh submission includes substantial longer-term data on multiple measures of neurologic benefit as well as additional long-term clinical data from current patients, the company said.

It also includes responses to the observations raised by the FDA in a letter to the company in July, Ultragenyx added.

Sanfilippo syndrome type A is a severe, progressive genetic disorder caused by a deficiency in the sulfamidase enzyme, leading to the buildup of heparan sulfate in cells, primarily damaging the brain and nervous system. Children with the disorder present with global developmental delay, which eventually leads to progressive cognitive, language and motor decline, behavioral abnormalities, and early death.

Previous Application

The FDA refused to approve the company’s application for the drug in July, and issued a letter requesting Ultragenyx to provide more information and improvements related to specific aspects of chemistry, manufacturing, and controls as well as observations from inspections of its manufacturing facility. The company then said it is working with the agency to resolve the observations.

Ultragenyx now expects a 6-month review period for the therapy and a likely decision from the FDA regarding potential approval by the third quarter of 2026. However, no date has been officially provided by the agency yet.

If approved, UX111 will be the first approved therapy for Sanfilippo syndrome type A, the company said.

How Did Stocktwits Users React?

On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around RARE stock stayed within the ‘bearish’ territory over the past 24 hours while message volume remained at ‘extremely low’ levels.

RARE stock fell by 46% over the past 12 months.

data.title: PureWave Hydrogen Corp. Provides Bi-Weekly Default Status Report Under National Policy 12-203
data.description:
(TheNewswire)

    Calgary, Alberta – TheNewswire - January 30, 2026 – PureWave HydrogenCorp. (the “Corporation”)(TSXV:PWH)  is providing this default status report in accordance withNational Policy 12-203 Management Cease Trade Orders (“NP 12-203”). 

    As previously announced on December 19, 2025, January6, 2026, and January 19, 2026, the Corporation was unable to file itsaudited financial statements for the year ended August 31, 2025,together with the related MD&A and CEO/CFO certificates(collectively, the “Required Filings”) by the prescribed deadlineof December 29, 2025. The Alberta Securities Commission (the“ASC”) granted a temporary management cease trade order(“MCTO”) on January 2, 2026, which remains in effect until theRequired Filings are completed and the MCTO is revoked.

    As of the date of this announcement, the Corporationconfirms that it is not subject to any insolvency proceedings. Otherthan as disclosed above, there is no other material informationconcerning the affairs of the Corporation that has not been generallydisclosed. The Corporation anticipates that it will be in a positionto remedy the default by filing the Required Filings on or beforeFebruary 27, 2026. The MCTO will remain in effect until the ASC hasrevoked the MCTO.  The Corporation also confirms that it expects itwill meet other prescribed continuous disclosure filing deadlines thatarise while the Required Filings remain outstanding and will provideupdates regarding any such anticipated subsequent specified defaultsin accordance with NP 12-203.

    The Corporation intends to satisfy the provisions ofthe alternative information guidelines under NP 12-203, including byissuing bi-weekly default status reports in the form of news releasesfor so long as the Required Filings remain outstanding.

    NEITHER THE TSX VENTURE EXCHANGE NORITS REGULATION SERVICES PROVIDER ACCEPTS RESPONSIBILITY FOR THEADEQUACY OR ACCURACY OF THIS RELEASE.

    For more information, pleasecontact:

    Bruce Nurse

    Director and Interim Chief Executive Officer

    Email: bruce.nurse@purewavehydrogen.com

    Phone: +1 (303) 919-2913

    Forward Looking Statements

    This news release contains forward-looking informationand forward-looking statements (collectively, “forward-looking statements”) within the meaning of applicable Canadian securitieslaws. Forward-looking statements are based on management’s currentexpectations and are subject to known and unknown risks,uncertainties, and other factors that may cause actual results todiffer materially. 1. data.title: REG - Helios Towers PLC - Total Voting Rights
data.description:
RNS Number : 1716R Helios Towers PLC 30 January 2026  

30 January 2026

Helios Towers plc

Total Voting Rights

In accordance with DTR 5.6.1, Helios Towers plc (the "Company") announces the following:

As at the date of this announcement, the Company's issued ordinary share capital consists of 1,042,201,422 ordinary shares of £0.01 each, all with voting rights. There are no shares held in treasury.

The total number of voting rights in the Company is therefore 1,042,201,422.

The above figure may be used by shareholders in the Company as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change of their interest in, the share capital of the Company under the FCA's Disclosure and Transparency Rules.

LEI: 213800DGC7GS4XCHCU30

Identification Code: GB00BJVQC708

Enquiries:

For investor enquiries

Chris Baker-Sams - Head of Strategic Finance and Investor Relations

+44 782 511 2288

investorrelations@heliostowers.com

For media enquiries

Headland

Andy Rivett-Carnac, +44 796 899 7365

Henry Wallers, +44 787 656 2436

Joe Hughes, +44 731 137 0016 

HeliosTowers@headlandconsultancy.com

About Helios Towers

·      Helios Towers is a leading independent mobile tower company connecting people and powering growth across Africa and the Middle East. We deliver world-class operations at nearly 15,000 mobile tower sites across nine countries in Africa and The Middle East - the fastest growing region globally for mobile services - providing mission critical infrastructure and power services to leading mobile network operators (MNOs).

·      Our pioneering approach enables colocation - the sharing of telecom tower sites - by hosting multiple MNOs on individual sites, creating benefits in the performance quality, the environmental impact, and the cost of rolling out and running mobile networks in our markets.

·      Helios Towers' business excellence methodology focuses on delivering world class performance for its customers - centred around the development and upskilling of its people. We foster a culture of learning and continuous improvement to deliver global standards in processes and innovation, which makes us the partner of choice for all the region's leading MNOs.

·      As one of the largest and fastest-growing FTSE-listed companies focused on operating in Africa and the Middle East, Helios Towers' disciplined approach to capital allocation, long-term partnerships with leading MNOs and its operational capabilities deliver resilient performance that is reshaping digital connectivity in the region and catalysing investment that is essential to unlocking its human and economic potential.

In accordance with the FCA's Disclosure and Transparency Rule 5.6.1R, Alfa Financial Software Holdings PLC ("Alfa" or the "Company"), a leading developer of mission-critical software for the asset finance industry, hereby notifies the market of the following:

As at 30 January 2026, the Company's issued ordinary share capital consists of 300,000,000 ordinary shares of 0.1p each, of which 3,351,689 are held in Treasury. The shares held in Treasury have no voting rights.  

Therefore, the total number of voting rights in the Company as at 30 January 2026 is 296,648,311.  Each share entitles the holder to one vote. 

The above figure may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine whether they are required to notify their interest in or a change to their interest in the Company under the Disclosure and Transparency Rules.

Teneo

James Macey White

Notes to Editors

Alfa has been delivering leading-edge technology to the global asset finance and leasing industry since 1990. Our specialised expertise enables us to deliver the most challenging systems transformation projects successfully.

Alfa Systems, our class-leading SaaS platform, is at the heart of the world's largest and most progressive asset finance operations. Supporting all types of automotive, equipment and wholesale finance, Alfa Systems is proven at volume and across borders, and trusted by leading brands to manage complex portfolios, drive efficiency and sustainability, and enhance the customer experience.

With full functionality for originations, servicing and collections, Alfa Systems is live in 37 countries, representing an integrated point solution, a rapid off-the-shelf implementation, or an end-to-end platform for the complex global enterprise. Alfa Systems 6, a breakthrough iteration of our software platform, was launched in 2024.

Alfa maintains exceptional customer satisfaction through an impeccable track record, with our experience and performance unrivalled in the industry. Our customers stick with us for the long term, because we deliver value that lasts for decades.

Alfa has offices all over Europe, Australasia and the Americas. For more information, visit us at alfasystems.com or on LinkedIn.

data.title: Gold Slides as Dollar Firms, Markets Weigh Warsh Fed Pick — Market Talk
data.description:
1139 ET - Gold prices extend losses amid a broader selloff across metals markets, pressured by a firmer U.S. dollar and profit-taking. Futures in New York fall 6% to $5,032 a troy ounce, though they remain on track for a weekly gain. Sentiment was also shaped by President Trump's announcement of Kevin Warsh as his pick to replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell. "The markets see Warsh as a more hawkish candidate than, for example, Kevin Hassett," says Thu Lan Nguyen from Commerzbank. However, "we continue to see a high probability that the central bank will yield to pressure to at least some extent and cut interest rates more than is currently priced in by the market." Meanwhile, the latest U.S. data showed prices charged by U.S. producers for their goods and services increased more than expected in December. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1052 ET - Signs that the Spanish economy might be running hot continue to emerge, as a buoyant jobs market drives labor costs higher, J.P. Morgan economist Mariana Monteiro says in a note. Spain's gross domestic product rose 0.8% in the final quarter of 2025, faster than consensus expectations and outperforming much of Europe. But while employment growth was strong, combined with a further rise in the work week, total hours worked grew faster than GDP, implying a significant productivity decline, she says. With falling productivity, the pickup in the annual growth of compensation per employee translates into a further rise in unit labour costs, Monteiro says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1029 ET - Atlanta Fed President Raphael Bostic reiterates his view that inflation remains too high and the Fed should return to its 2% target in a CNBC interview. "We are still too high on inflation, so I think we need to be somewhat restrictive," he said. Bostic's comments come after the Fed held rates steady Wednesday, which followed what Bostic said was his last meeting before his retirement at the end of February. Bostic expects there will be some tariff impacts into the economy in the first half of this year. Bostic says that while he doesn't know President Trump's pick for the new chair, Kevin Warsh, very well, he has heard he's thoughtful and "someone who you can really engage with." (jessica.coacci@wsj.com; jessica_coacci)

1028 ET - With underlying momentum for Canada's economy still too weak to sustainably reduce slack, it is likely that interest rates in the country will need to remain in stimulative territory throughout this year and into the start of 2027. CIBC Capital Economics' Andrew Grantham argues. That puts him on the same page as many other economists anticipating an extended hold for the Bank of Canada. Grantham says the economy was still struggling to eke out growth toward the end of 4Q 2025, leaving activity tracking slightly below the central bank's projections, though not by enough to revive discussions of further interest rate cuts. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1025 ET - Canada's economy flatlined in November and picked up only slightly in December, leaving a weak 4Q that is nonetheless unlikely to faze the Bank of Canada, Scotiabank's Derek Holt says. The economist notes there was a convoluted mess of macro drivers, weather and strikes that affected 4Q activity but it's unclear the numbers will mildly surprise the central bank given it has strongly signalled it is looking through what was and toward what may be.(robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1024 ET - It is increasingly clear Canadian GDP probably declined in 4Q, Capital Economics' Alexandra Brown says. Monthly data for November showing flat industry-level GDP is grimmer than it first appears, with broad based declines in goods producing industries offset by a temporary boost from service-sector strikes ending, the economist says. And the flash estimate for only weak growth in December reduced the change quarter growth will rebound sharply by 1.8% annualized in 1Q 2026 as the Bank of Canada expects. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1022 ET - Canada's economy hit the brakes in 4Q 2025 and may have slipped into reverse but will likely avoid a technical recession in the near term, reckons Oxford Economics' Tony Stillo. He expects U.S. tariffs, elevated trade policy uncertainty and a shrinking population will keep Canadian GDP growth sluggish until the latter half of 2026. And it also likely means the Bank of Canada remains on hold for all of 2026, he adds. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1005 ET - The difficult adjustment for Canada's economy to trade uncertainty continues, with manufacturing, wholesale and administrative support industries contracting month-over-month in November, says Manulife Investment Management's Dominique Lapointe. The latest data means GDP is set for a renewed contraction in 4Q 2025. Lapointe notes President Trump's threat overnight to decertify all planes made in Canada and impose a 50% tariff on new Canadian planes sold in the U.S. only adds to uncertainty and reinforces his conviction that a negotiated free trade deal looks increasingly unlikely in 2026. He adds market pricing is currently broadly in line with his forecast for no change in the Bank of Canada's policy interest rate this year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1002 ET - The latest Canadian GDP report shows that economic activity remains modest and that the recovery from the hit from U.S. tariffs in 2Q 2025 remains modest and uncertain, Servus Credit Union's Charles St-Arnaud says. Economic activity ended 2025 slightly lower than its peak in July, and the economist says there is little indication that a sustainable pickup in activity is about to take place. Still, with the labor market remaining resilient, Canada's economy isn't deteriorating either, St-Arnaud says. And that supports our view Bank of Canada will remain on hold for an extended period, while standing ready to act if the economy shows signs of deterioration. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0957 ET - So much for the Canadian economy's rebound. A flash estimates for another muted GDP performance in December after industry-level surprised to the downside in November adds a dovish tone to the latest Canadian economic data and pushed 4Q GDP tracking further below the Bank of Canada's projections, strategists at TD Securities say. The country is now tracking toward a 0.5% annualized quarter-on-quarter contraction, though TD says it likely will take a much larger shock for the central bank to respond. The GDP report was largely a non-event in terms of market reaction, though the strategists say consecutive dovish surprises may start to weigh on the market in the future. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0955 ET - President Trump's decision to nominate a former Federal Reserve governor to lead the central bank as opposed to an outsider has helped stabilize the dollar, Macquarie Group strategist Thierry Wizman says in a note. Traders might assume that Trump wants to send a message of continuity in the Fed's institutional traditions by nominating Kevin Warsh as Fed Chair. However, Trump has "unlikely strayed from his own belief system," having put pressure on the Fed to cut rates. Warsh is a family friend of Trump and shadowed the president on monetary policy since 2009. That means when the time to cut rates approaches, Warsh will be quicker to do so, he says. The DXY dollar index rises 0.4% to 96.667. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0935 ET - The Polish economy is set for more strength ahead following a solid year, Leszek Kasek and Mateusz Sutowicz at ING say in a note. GDP increased by 3.6% in 2025, compared with 3.0% a year prior. Growth was more balanced than in 2024, boosted by public and private consumption. Investments rose 4.2%, adding 0.7% to GDP, after dragging growth by 0.2% a year prior. "December data from the Polish economy suggest that economic growth is becoming increasingly diversified and is starting to rely more heavily on investment," the analysts say. ING estimates that growth sped up to around 4% in the fourth quarter, and forecasts an acceleration in 2026 to 3.7%. "The continued increase in private consumption should be accompanied by a further rebound in investment," they say. (don.forbes@wsj.com)

data.title: RedBird Capital Partners Completes Refinancing of AC Milan
data.description:
New Debt Facilities Create Long-Term Financial Flexibility and Eliminate Loans Related to the Acquisition of the Club in 2022

NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--January 30, 2026--

RedBird Capital Partners today announced the successful completion of a refinancing that strengthens AC Milan's capital structure and long-term financial flexibility.

The transaction refinances the acquisition-related vendor financing originally provided by entities advised by Elliott Advisors UK Limited ("Elliott") in connection with RedBird's acquisition of AC Milan and replaces it with new institutional debt financing arranged by Comvest Credit Partners.

The refinancing aligns the Club's capital structure with RedBird's long-term strategic and operational objectives for AC Milan as the club's sole control owner. As part of the transaction, Elliott Managing Partner Gordon Singer and Associate Portfolio Manager Dominic Mitchell will step down from the AC Milan Board. No other changes have been made to the Board or Management team, preserving continuity in the Club's governance and operational oversight.

Gerry Cardinale, RedBird's Managing Partner and President of AC Milan, said, "Over the past three years, we have worked to consistently strengthen AC Milan's financial and sporting performance, returning the Club to profitability and building a strong, more sustainable foundation for its future. Our ownership of AC Milan is driven by a long-term commitment to the Club, its leadership in the city of Milan and its heritage as one of the most successful European football clubs in the world. With the San Siro project now underway, we are even more confident in the Club's trajectory and our ability to support its continued growth and success. We will continue to identify opportunities to enhance value while elevating AC Milan and the city of Milan on the world stage."

Gordon Singer said, "We are proud of what AC Milan has accomplished since Elliott acquired the club in 2018. Under Elliott's ownership and subsequently RedBird's, the club's financial and sporting performance have improved significantly and the team has won two major trophies, including the 2021/22 Scudetto. The stewardship of a historic institution like AC Milan is an important responsibility, and we wish RedBird continued success in the future."

About RedBird Capital Partners

RedBird Capital Partners is a private investment firm that builds high-growth companies and provides strategic capital solutions to founders and entrepreneurs. Founded in 2014 by Gerry Cardinale, RedBird integrates sophisticated private equity investing with a hands-on business building mandate, focusing on two core ecosystems — Sports, Media & Entertainment and Financial Services. The firm currently manages over $14 billion in assets on behalf of a global group of blue chip institutional and family office investors. For more information, please go to www.redbirdcap.com.

About AC Milan

Founded in 1899 in Milan, Italy, AC Milan is one of the most iconic and historic brands in global sports. In its 125-year history, AC Milan's on-the-pitch success has been marked by 7 Champions League titles (2nd most in European football), 19 Serie A titles, 5 UEFA Super Cup (record: tied) and a FIFA World Cup. The Club currently plays its home games at San Siro Stadium in Milan and is developing a new 70,000 seat stadium that will be one of the most advanced modern sports and entertainment complexes in the world. With its sporting accomplishments and iconic red and black striped jersey, AC Milan is among the most recognized sporting brands in the world, with over 550 million fans globally. In August 2022, RedBird Capital Partners, a New-York based private equity firm founded and led by Gerry Cardinale, became the controlling owner of the Club. As part of that acquisition, RedBird brought in its long-time partner the New York Yankees as a minority investor. AC Milan has offices in Milan and Dubai. For more information, please visit www.acmilan.com.

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260130940582/en/

CONTACT: Dan Gagnier

Gagnier Communications

RedBird@gagnierfc.com
